history: The client recently completed an undergraduate degree and is entering the workforce, having moved to New York to explore their identity away from family influence. They came out to their parents within the last two and a half years and received negative reactions, leading to increased distance and strained relationships, particularly with their mother and sister. The client describes their mother as focused on practical concerns rather than emotional support, and their sister as envious of the client's independence. The client reports a pattern of rushing through tasks, pervasive anxiety, sleep disturbances, and feelings of loneliness despite being surrounded by people. Losses of significant relationships, such as the death of a husband and mother, contribute to a sense of loneliness and overwhelm.
unlovable_belief: I am bound to be abandoned
intermediate_belief: If I show my true self or express my needs, others will distance themselves or react negatively, so I must keep my feelings to myself to avoid further hurt. If I don't keep control over my life and future, things will fall apart and I will be left alone. If I don't keep busy and push worries away, I will be overwhelmed by my emotions.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I can't make my family accept me or feel connected, it means I am fundamentally unlovable and things will never get better. If I can't control my worries or loneliness, I will always feel this empty and overwhelmed.
coping_strategies: The client copes by pushing worries and emotions to the back of their mind, rushing through tasks to avoid sitting with distress, and maintaining distance from family to protect themselves from further rejection. They seek reassurance from advisors but find it only temporarily helpful, and struggle with sleep disturbances that worsen their mood.
situation: Recalling the experience of coming out to parents and the negative aftermath. Preparing for or having interactions with family while maintaining distance.
auto_thought: They'll never really accept me for who I am. No matter what I do, things will always be tense and I can't be myself around them.
behavior: I avoid deep conversations with my family and keep interactions brief or focused on practical matters. I keep my distance emotionally and physically, and distract myself by staying busy or pushing thoughts away.
